documentation:
[![Documentation Status](https://readthedocs.org/projects/marsilea/badge/?version=stable&style=flat-square)](https://marsilea.readthedocs.io/en/stable) ![pypi version](https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/marsilea?color=0098FF&logo=python&logoColor=white&style=flat-square) ![Conda Version](https://img.shields.io/conda/vn/conda-forge/marsilea?style=flat-square&logo=anaconda&logoColor=white&color=%2344A833) ![PyPI - License](https://img.shields.io/pypi/l/marsilea?color=FFD43B&style=flat-square) # Marsilea: Declarative creation of composable visualization! --- ## Documentation You can read the documentation on Read the Docs. [Read Documentation](https://marsilea.readthedocs.io/) ## Installation ### Using PIP ```shell pip install marsilea ``` ### Using Conda/Mamba ```shell conda install -c conda-forge marsilea # or mamba install -c conda-forge marsilea ``` ## What is Composable Visualization?

When we do visualization, we often need to combine multiple plots to show different aspects of the data. For example, we may need to create a heatmap to show the expression of genes in different cells, and then create a bar chart to show the expression of genes in different cell types. A visualization contains multiple plots is called a composable visualization. In Marsilea, we employ a declarative approach for user to create composable visualization incrementally. ## Examples
Bar Chart With Image Stacked Bar Arc Diagram
Heatmap Oncoprint Upsetplot

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.area.Area
documentation:
Area plot

# Parameters

data

: The data to be plotted.

color

: The color of the area

add_outline

: Whether to add outline to the area

alpha

: The transparency of the area

linecolor

: The color of the outline

linewidth

: The width of the outline

group_kws

: The configurations that apply to each group

kwargs :

: Additional configurations for the area plot, see {func}`matplotlib.pyplot.fill_between`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    import numpy as np
    import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

    from marsilea.plotter import Area

    _, ax = plt.subplots()
    data = np.random.randint(0, 10, 10) + 1
    Area(data).render(ax)
```

classes.name: marsilea.layers.LayersMesh
documentation:
The mesh that draw customized elements in multi-layers

LayersMesh is a powerful solution for drawing and visualizing customized
elements in multi-layered formats.
It offers two flexible ways to specify layers:

1. A single layer of data containing different elements.
2. Multiple layers of data, where each layer represents a customized element, overlaying elements on top of one another.

When supplying multiple layers,
the drawing order follows the order of the input array.
Override this order by controlling the `zorder` attribute in your {class}`Pieces`.

# Parameters

data

: Use this parameter for a single layer

layers

: For multiple layers, use this parameter.
  Each layer must be a boolean matrix indicating
  whether the element is rendered at a specific cell.

pieces

: For a single layer, use a dict to define how to render each element.
  For multiple layers, use an array to define the rendering of each layer.

label

: The mesh label, displayed only when added to the side plot.

label_loc

: The label location.

props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

# Examples

Drawing a single layer:

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from marsilea.layers import LayersMesh, Rect, FrameRect
    >>> data = np.random.choice([1, 2, 3], (10, 10))
    >>> pieces = {1: Rect(color="r", label="1"),
    ...           2: FrameRect(color="g", label="2"),
    ...           3: Rect(color="b", label="3")}
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> LayersMesh(data=data, pieces=pieces).render(ax)
```

Drawing multiple layers:

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> d1 = np.random.choice([1, 2, 3], (10, 10))
    >>> d2 = np.random.choice([1, 2, 3], (10, 10))
    >>> d3 = np.random.choice([1, 2, 3], (10, 10))
    >>> pieces = [Rect(color="r", label="1"),
    ...           FrameRect(color="g", label="2"),
    ...           Rect(color="b", label="3")]
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> LayersMesh(layers=[d1, d2, d3], pieces=pieces).render(ax)
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.arc.Arc
documentation:
Arc diagram.

# Parameters

anchors

: Anchors of the arcs.

links

: Links of the arcs.

weights

: Weights of the links. If not specified, all links will have the same
  width.

width

: Width of the links. If `weights` is specified, `width` must be a tuple
  of two floats, where the first float is the minimum width and the
  second float is the maximum width. If `weights` is not specified,
  `width` must be a float.

colors

: Colors of the links. If not specified, all links will have the same
  color.

labels

: Labels of the links. If not specified, no legend will be shown.

legend_kws

: Keyword arguments passed to `legendkit.cat_legend`.

label

: Label of the plot.

label_loc

: Location of the label. Must be one of the following:
  "left", "right", "top", "bottom", "center".

label_props

: Keyword arguments passed to `matplotlib.text.Text`.

kwargs

: Keyword arguments passed to `matplotlib.patches.Arc`.

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import Arc
    >>> anchors = np.arange(10)
    >>> links = [(0, 7), (1, 5), (3, 10), (0, 9), (5, 8), (2, 6), (4, 8)]
    >>> weights = [1, 2, 3, 5, 2, 1, 2]
    >>> colors = ["C0", "C1", "C2", "C3", "C4", "C5", "C6"]
    >>> labels = ["A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F", "G"]
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(np.random.rand(10, 10))
    >>> h.add_top(Arc(anchors, links, weights=weights, colors=colors, labels=labels))
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.range.Range
documentation:
Range plot

The range plot shows the range between two categories.
The input data should be a DataFrame with two columns.

# Parameters

data

: The input data.

items

: The names of the items.

marker

: The marker style.

markersize

: The size of the marker.

color1

: The color of the first range.

color2

: The color of the second range.

edgecolor1

: The edge color of the first range.

edgecolor2

: The edge color of the second range.

edgewidth

: The width of the edge.

linecolor

: The color of the line.

linewidth

: The width of the line.

label

: The label of the plot.

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 2)
    >>> range_data = np.random.randint(1, 100, (10, 2))
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> h.add_left(ma.plotter.Range(range_data, items=["A", "B"]))
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.bar.Numbers
documentation:
Show numbers in bar plot

This is used to show 1D data specifically.

# Parameters

data

: 1D data

width

: The width of bar

color

: The color of bar

show_value

: Whether to show value on the bar

fmt

: Format the value show on the bar

label

: The label of the plot

value_pad

: The spacing between label and the plot

props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import Numbers
    >>> data = np.random.randint(1, 10, 10)
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> Numbers(data).render(ax)
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.bar.CenterBar
documentation:
Two comparable bar plots with center line

# Parameters

data

: If input with array, must be 2D array with shape (n, 2).
  If input with DataFrame, must have two columns.

names

: The names of the two bars

width

: The width of bar

colors

: The colors of the two bars

orient

: The orientation of the plot

show_value

: Whether to show value on the bar

fmt

: Format the value show on the bar

label

: The label of the plot

value_pad

: The spacing between value and the plot

props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

kwargs:

: Other keyword arguments passed to {meth}`matplotlib.axes.Axes.bar`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import CenterBar
    >>> data = np.random.randint(1, 10, (10, 2))
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> CenterBar(data, names=["Bar 1", "Bar 2"]).render(ax)

```

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> CenterBar(data, names=["Bar 1", "Bar 2"], orient="h").render(ax)
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.bar.StackBar
documentation:
Stacked Bar

# Parameters

data

: 2D data, index of dataframe is used as the name of items.

items

: The name of items.

colors

: The colors of the bar for each item.

orient

: The orientation of the plot

show_value

: Whether to show value on the bar

value_loc

: The location of the value

fmt

: Format the value show on the bar

label

: The label of the plot

value_pad

: The spacing between value and the bar

props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

kwargs :

: Other keyword arguments passed to {meth}`matplotlib.axes.Axes.bar`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from marsilea.plotter import StackBar
    >>> stack_data = pd.DataFrame(
    ...     data=np.random.randint(1, 10, (5, 10)), index=list("abcde")
    ... )
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> StackBar(stack_data).render(ax)

```

You may find the text is too big for a bar to display on, to not display certain value.

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> fmt = lambda v: int(v) if v > 2 else ""
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> StackBar(stack_data, show_value=True, fmt=fmt).render(ax)
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.text.AnnoLabels
documentation:
Annotate a few rows or columns

This is useful when your heatmap contains many rows/columns,
and you only want to annotate a few of them.

# Parameters

labels

: The length of the labels should match the main canvas side where
  it attaches to, the labels that won't be displayed should be masked.

mark

: If your labels is not a mask array, this will help you mark the labels
  that you want to draw

side : str
text_pad : float

> Add extra space and the start and end of the label

text_gap

: Add extra spacing between the labels, relative to the fontsize

pointer_size

: The size of the pointer in inches

linewidth

: The linewidth of the pointer

connectionstyle :
relpos : 2-tuple
armA, armB : float
label : str

> The label of the plot

label_loc

: The location of the label

label_props

: The label properties

options :

: Pass to {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> labels = np.arange(100)

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import AnnoLabels
    >>> matrix = np.random.randn(100, 10)
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(matrix)
    >>> marks = AnnoLabels(labels, mark=[3, 4, 5, 96, 97, 98])
    >>> h.add_right(marks)
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.text.Labels

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.text.Labels._align_compact
documentation: Make align keyword compatible to any side

Add text labels

# Parameters

labels : array of str
align : str

> Which side of the text to align

padding

: The buffer space between text and the adjcent plots, in points unit

text_props

: A dict of array that control the text properties for each text.

label

: The label of the plot

label_loc

: The location of the label

label_props

: The label properties

options

: Pass to {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

# Examples

To set the text properties for each text, use {code}`text_props`

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> labels = np.arange(20)
    >>> colors = ["r" if labels % 2 else "b" for labels in labels]

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import Labels
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(np.random.randn(20, 20))
    >>> h.add_right(Labels(labels, text_props={"color": colors}))
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.text.Title

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.text.Title._align_compact
documentation: Make align keyword compatible to any side

Add a title

# Parameters

title

: The title text

align

: Where the title is placed

padding

: The buffer space between text and the adjcent plots, in points unit

fontsize

: The title font size

rotation :
options : dict

> Pass to {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

# See Also

> {meth}`marsilea.base.WhiteBoard.add_title`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import Title
    >>> matrix = np.random.randn(15, 10)
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(matrix)
    >>> for align in ["left", "right", "center"]:
    ...     title = Title(f"Title align={align}", align=align)
    ...     h.add_top(title)
    >>> for align in ["top", "bottom", "center"]:
    ...     title = Title(f"Title align={align}", align=align)
    ...     h.add_left(title)
    >>> h.render()
```

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.text._ChunkBase._align_compact
documentation: Make align keyword compatible to any side

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.text.Chunk
documentation:
Mark groups

This is useful to mark each groups after you split the plot,
the order of the chunks will align with cluster order.

# Parameters

texts

: The label for each chunk

fill_colors

: The color used as background color for each chunk

borderwidth, bordercolor, borderstyle :

: Control the style of border, you can pass an array to style each group.
  For borderstyle, see {meth}`linestyles <matplotlib.lines.Line2D.set_linestyle>`

props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

rotation

: How many angle to rotate the text coutner-clockwise, in degree unit

padding

: The buffer space between text and the adjcent plots, in points unit

label

: The label of the plot

label_loc

: The location of the label

label_props

: The label properties

# See Also

> {class}`FixedChunk`

# Examples

The order of chunk will be aligned with the order of cluster.

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import Chunk
    >>> matrix = np.random.randn(20, 20)
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(matrix)
    >>> chunk = ["C1", "C2", "C3", "C4"]
    >>> labels = np.random.choice(chunk, size=20)
    >>> h.group_rows(labels, order=chunk)
    >>> h.add_right(Chunk(chunk, bordercolor="gray"), pad=0.1)
    >>> h.add_dendrogram("left")
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.text.FixedChunk
documentation:
Mark groups with fixed order

# Parameters

texts

: The label for each chunk

fill_colors

: The color used as background color for each chunk

ratio

: To span chunks on more than one chunk.

borderwidth, bordercolor, borderstyle :

: Control the style of border
  For borderstyle, see {meth}`linestyles <matplotlib.lines.Line2D.set_linestyle>`

props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

rotation

: How many to rotate the text

padding

: The buffer space between text and the adjcent plots, in points unit

label

: The label of the plot

label_loc

: The location of the label

label_props

: The label properties

# See Also

> {class}`Chunk`

# Examples

The fixed chunk will not be reordered by the cluster results.

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import FixedChunk
    >>> matrix = np.random.randn(20, 20)
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(matrix)
    >>> chunk = ["C1", "C2", "C3", "C4"]
    >>> labels = np.random.choice(chunk, size=20)
    >>> h.group_rows(labels, order=chunk)
    >>> h.add_right(FixedChunk(chunk, bordercolor="gray"), pad=0.1)
    >>> h.add_dendrogram("left")
    >>> h.render()
```

You can span a chunk on more than one chunk.

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(matrix)
    >>> chunk = ["C1", "C2-1", "C2-2", "C4"]
    >>> labels = np.random.choice(chunk, size=20)
    >>> h.group_rows(labels, order=chunk)
    >>> h.add_right(FixedChunk(chunk, bordercolor="gray"), pad=0.1)
    >>> h.add_right(
    ...     FixedChunk(
    ...         ["C1", "C2", "C3"],
    ...         fill_colors="red",
    ...         ratio=[1, 2, 1],
    ...     ),
    ...     pad=0.1,
    ... )
    >>> h.render()
```

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.base.RenderPlan._get_split_render_spec
documentation: def get_render_data(self):
data = self.get_data()
if self.has_deform:
self.get_deform_func()
else:
if len(data) == 1:
return data[0]
else:
return data

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.base.RenderPlan.is_split
documentation:
For the RenderPlan to self-aware of whether its render canvas
will be split. Useful to determine how to get render data.

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.base.RenderPlan.render_ax
documentation:
The major rendering function
Define how the plot is drawn

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.base.RenderPlan.render
documentation:
This function will be call when render a plot

- If the canvas is split, {meth}`render_axes` will be called.
- If only one ax is passed, {meth}`render_ax` will be called.

```python
if self.is_split:
    self.render_axes(axes)
else:
    self.render_ax(axes, self.get_render_data())
```

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.base.RenderPlan.get_canvas_size
documentation:
If the size is unknown before rendering, this function must be
implemented to return the canvas size in inches.

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.base.RenderPlan.get_legends
documentation:
This should define the legend return by the RenderPlan.

The return object could be any {class}`matplotlib.artist.Artist`

:::{note}
{class}`matplotlib.colorbar.Colorbar` is not an {class}`Artist`.
Use {class}`legendkit.colorart`
:::

The base class for every plotter in Marsilea

Attributes
----------
name : str
    The name of the plot,
    can be used to retrieve the render axes and corresponding legend.
side : str, default: 'top'
    Which side to render this plot
size : float
allow_split : bool, default: True
    Use to mark if the RenderPlan can be split
render_main : bool, default: False
    Use to mark if the RenderPlan can be rendered on main canvas
zorder : int, default: 0
    This only works if the RenderPlan is rendered on main canvas

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.base.StatsBase
documentation: A base class for rendering statistics plot

modules.name: marsilea.plotter.images
documentation: The implementation of Image in matplotlib may suffer from compatibility
issues across different rendering backend at different DPI. Currently
not a public API.

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.images.Image
documentation:
Plot static images

# Parameters

images

: You can input either path to the image, URL, or numpy array.

align

: The alignment of the images.

scale

: The scale of the images.

spacing

: The spacing between images, a value between 0 and 1,
  relative to the image container size.

resize

: The size to resize the images.

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> c = ma.ZeroWidth(height=2)
    >>> c.add_right(
    ...     ma.plotter.Image(
    ...         [
    ...             "https://www.iconfinder.com/icons/4375050/download/png/512",
    ...             "https://www.iconfinder.com/icons/8666426/download/png/512",
    ...             "https://www.iconfinder.com/icons/652581/download/png/512",
    ...         ],
    ...         align="right",
    ...     ),
    ...     pad=0.1,
    ... )
    >>> c.add_right(
    ...     ma.plotter.Labels(["Python", "Rust", "JavaScript"], fontsize=20), pad=0.1
    ... )
    >>> c.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.images.Emoji
documentation:
Have fun with emoji images

The emoji images are from [twemoji](https://twemoji.twitter.com/).

You can will all twemoji from [here](https://twemoji-cheatsheet.vercel.app/)

# Parameters

codes

: The emoji codes. You can input either unicode or short code.

lang

: The language of the emoji.

scale

: The scale of the emoji.

spacing

: The spacing between emoji, a value between 0 and 1,
  relative to the emoji container size.

# Examples

```
.. plot::

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> c = ma.ZeroHeight(width=2)
    >>> c.add_top(ma.plotter.Emoji("😆😆🤣😂😉😇🐍🦀🦄"))
    >>> c.render()
```

functions.name: marsilea.plotter.mesh.encode_numeric
documentation: transform input data to numeric

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.mesh.ColorMesh

methods.name: marsilea.plotter.mesh.ColorMesh._annotate_text
documentation: Add textual labels with the value in each cell.

Continuous Heatmap

# Parameters

data

: 2D data

cmap

: The colormap used to map the value to the color

norm

: A Normalize instance to map data

vmin, vmax

: Value to determine the value mapping behavior

center

: The value to center on colormap, notice that this is different from
  seaborn; If set, a {class}`matplotlib.colors.TwoSlopeNorm` will be used
  to center the colormap.

mask

: A bool matrix indicates which cell will be masked and not rendered

alpha

: The transparency value

linewidth

: The width of grid line

linecolor

: The color of grid line

annot

: Whether to show the value / The text to show in each cell

fmt

: The format string or a function to format the value

annot_kws

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

cbar_kws

: See {class}`matplotlib.colorbar.Colorbar`,

label

: The label of the plot, only show if added to the side plot

label_loc

: Where to add the label

label_props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

kwargs :

# See Also

> {class}`marsilea.Heatmap`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import ColorMesh
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 0.5))
    >>> ColorMesh(np.arange(10), cmap="Blues").render(ax)
```

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> data = np.random.randn(10, 8)
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> h.cut_rows(cut=[5])
    >>> h.add_dendrogram("left")
    >>> cmap1, cmap2 = "Purples", "Greens"
    >>> colors1 = ColorMesh(np.arange(10) + 1, cmap=cmap1, label=cmap1, annot=True)
    >>> colors2 = ColorMesh(np.arange(10) + 1, cmap=cmap2, label=cmap2)
    >>> h.add_right(colors1, size=0.2, pad=0.05)
    >>> h.add_right(colors2, size=0.2, pad=0.05)
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.mesh.Colors
documentation:
Categorical colors/heatmap

# Parameters

data : np.ndarray
label : str
label_loc : {'top', 'bottom', 'left', 'right'}
palette : dict, array-like

> Could be a mapping of label and colors or an array match
> the shape of data

cmap

: The colormap used to map the value to the color

mask

: Indicate which cell will be masked and not rendered

label

: The label of the plot, only show if added to the side plot

label_loc

: Where to add the label

label_props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

legend_kws :

: See {class}`legendkit.legend`

kwargs :

: Pass to {meth}`pcolormesh <matplotlib.axes.Axes.pcolormesh>`

# See Also

> {class}`marsilea.CatHeatmap`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import Colors
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 0.5))
    >>> data = np.random.choice(["A", "B", "C"], 10)
    >>> Colors(data).render(ax)
```

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(np.random.randn(10, 8))
    >>> h.cut_rows(cut=[5])
    >>> h.add_dendrogram("left")
    >>> color = Colors(data, label="Colors")
    >>> h.add_right(color, size=0.2, pad=0.05)
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.mesh.SizedMesh
documentation:
Mesh for sized elements

:::{note}
If encode color as categorical data, `palette` must be used
to assign color for each category.
:::

# Parameters

size

: Control the radius of circles, must be numeric

color: color-like or 2d array

: The color of circles, could be numeric / categorical matrix
  If using one color name, all circles will have the same color.

cmap

: The colormap used to map the value to the color

norm

: A Normalize instance to map data

vmin, vmax

: Value to determine the value mapping behavior

center

: The value to center on colormap, notice that this is different from
  seaborn; If set, a {class}`matplotlib.colors.TwoSlopeNorm` will be used
  to center the colormap.

alpha

: The transparency value

sizes

: The range of the size of elements

size_norm

: A Normalize instance to map size

edgecolor

: The border color of each elements

linewidth

: The width of the border of each elements

frameon

: Whether to draw a frame

grid

: Whether to draw grid

grid_color

: The color of grid

grid_linewidth

: The width of grid line

palette

: Use to map color if input categorical data

marker

: See {mod}`matplotlib.markers`

label

: The label of the plot, only show if added to the side plot

label_loc

: Where to add the label

label_props

: See {class}`matplotlib.text.Text`

legend

: Whether to add a legend

size_legend_kws

: Control the size legend, See {class}`legendkit.size_legend`

color_legend_kws

: Control the color legend, See {class}`legendkit.colorart`

kwargs

: Pass to {meth}`matplotlib.axes.Axes.scatter`

# See Also

> {class}`marsilea.SizedHeatmap`

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import SizedMesh
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 0.5))
    >>> size, color = np.random.rand(1, 10), np.random.rand(1, 10)
    >>> SizedMesh(size, color).render(ax)
```

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(np.random.randn(10, 8))
    >>> h.cut_rows(cut=[5])
    >>> h.add_dendrogram("left")
    >>> mesh = SizedMesh(size, color, marker="*", label="SizedMesh")
    >>> h.add_right(mesh, size=0.2, pad=0.05)
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.plotter.bio.SeqLogo
documentation:
Sequence logo

# Parameters

matrix

: Drawing matrix, the data is the height of logo
  with columns as positions and rows as nucleotides/amino acids letters.

width

: The width of each letter, by default .9, should be within [0, 1]

color_encode

: The color encoding for each letter,
  should be a dict with key as letter and value as color

stack

: The stacking order of letters, by height. If normal will stack
  as the order in matrix.

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import pandas as pd
    >>> from marsilea.plotter import SeqLogo
    >>> matrix = pd.DataFrame(
    ...     data=np.random.randint(1, 10, (4, 10)), index=list("ACGT")
    ... )
    >>> _, ax = plt.subplots()
    >>> colors = {"A": "r", "C": "b", "G": "g", "T": "black"}
    >>> SeqLogo(matrix, color_encode=colors).render(ax)
```

methods.name: marsilea.upset.UpsetData.from_sets
documentation:
Create UpsetData from a series of sets

Parameters
----------
sets : array of sets, dict
    The sets data
sets_names : optional
    The name of sets, if name is not provided, it will
    be automatically named as "Set 1, Set 2, ..."
sets_attrs : optional, pd.DataFrame
    The attributes of sets, the input index should be the
    same as sets_names
items_attrs : optional, pd.DataFrame
    The attributes of items, the input index should be the
    same as items

methods.name: marsilea.upset.UpsetData.from_memberships
documentation:
Describe the sets an item are in

Parameters
----------
items : array of array of sets_names, dict
    The data of items
items_names : optional
    The name of items, if name is not provided, it will
    be automatically named as "Item 1, Item 2, ..."
sets_attrs : optional, pd.DataFrame
    The attributes of sets, the input index should be the
    same as sets_names
items_attrs : optional, pd.DataFrame
    The attributes of items, the input index should be the
    same as items

methods.name: marsilea.upset.UpsetData.has_item
documentation: Return a list of sets' name the item is in

methods.name: marsilea.upset.UpsetData.intersection
documentation: Return the items that are shared in different sets

methods.name: marsilea.upset.UpsetData.intersection_count
documentation: The item has occurred in how many sets

methods.name: marsilea.upset.UpsetData.cardinality
documentation: The number of items in intersections

methods.name: marsilea.upset.UpsetData.degree
documentation: Intersection between how many sets

methods.name: marsilea.upset.UpsetData.get_items_attr
documentation:
Return the attribute of items in the order of plotting

Parameters
----------
attr : key in items_attrs
    Retrieve the attribute of items

Returns
-------
    array of attribute

Handle multiple sets

Normally, the construction methods are used to create a UpsetData

Terminology that you might not be familiar with:
: - set: a collection of unique items
  - subset: intersection of sets
  - degree: The number of sets that intersect with each other
  - cardinality: The number of items in the subset

# Parameters

data

: A one-hot encode matrix indicates if an item is in a set.
  Columns are sets and rows are items

sets_names

: The name of sets

items

: The name of items

sets_attrs

: The attributes of sets, the input index should be the
  same as names

items_attrs

: The attributes of items, the input index should be the
  same as items

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    from marsilea.upset import UpsetData
    sets = [[1,2,3,4], [3,4,5,6]]
    data = UpsetData.from_sets(sets)
```

     The maximum cardinality of the subset

    The maximum degree of the subset

methods.name: marsilea.upset.Upset.add_sets_attr
documentation:
Add a plot for the sets attribute

Parameters
----------
side : str
    The side to add the plot, can be 'left', 'right', 'top', 'bottom'
attr_name : str
    The name of the attribute
plot : str
    The type of plot, can be 'bar', 'box', 'boxen', 'violin', 'point',
    'strip', 'swarm', 'stack_bar', 'number'
name : str, optional
    The name of the plot
pad : float, optional
    The padding between the plot and the axis
size : float, optional
    The size of the plot
plot_kws : dict, optional
    The keyword arguments for the plot

methods.name: marsilea.upset.Upset.add_items_attr
documentation:
Add a plot for the items attribute

Parameters
----------
side : str
    The side to add the plot, can be 'left', 'right', 'top', 'bottom'
attr_name : str
    The name of the attribute
plot : str
    The type of plot, can be 'bar', 'box', 'boxen', 'violin', 'point',
    'strip', 'swarm', 'stack_bar', 'number'
name : str, optional
    The name of the plot
pad : float, optional
    The padding between the plot and the axis
size : float, optional
    The size of the plot
plot_kws : dict, optional
    The keyword arguments for the plot

Upset Plot

# Parameters

data

: Upset data

orient

: The orientation of the Upset plot

sets_order

: The order of sets

sets_color

: The color for each set, this will also change the bar color
  in set size

sort_sets

: The sorting order of sets

sort_subsets

: How to sort the subset, 'size' will sort by intersection size,
  'degree' will sort by the number of intersected sets.

min_degree, max_degree

: Select a fraction of subset to render by degree

min_cardinality, max_cardinality

: Select a fraction of subset to render by cardinality

color

: The main color to use

shading

: The value to dilute the main color

radius

: The size of the dot

linewidth

: The width of lines that connect the dots

grid_background

: The value to dilute the main color for background

fontsize

: Set the fontsize for the plot

add_intersections

: Whether or which side to add the intersection size.

add_sets_size

: Whether or which side to add the sets size.

add_labels

: Whether or which side to add the label.

width : float
height : float
size_scale : float

> The scale of the plot

size_aspect

: The aspect ratio of the plot

# Examples

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> from marsilea.upset import UpsetData, Upset
    >>> data = UpsetData.from_sets([[1, 2, 3, 4],
    >>>                             [3, 4, 5, 6],
    >>>                             [1, 6, 10, 11]])
    >>> Upset(data).render()
```

functions.name: marsilea.dataset.load_data
documentation:
To load marsilea dataset

- `cooking_oils`: Cooking oils dataset
- `imdb`: The IMDB Top 100 Movies
- `pbmc3k`: single-cell RNA-seq dataset from 10X Genomics
- `oncoprint`: Subsample of Breast Invasive Carcinoma (TCGA, PanCancer Atlas)
- `mouse_embryo`: Spatial mapping of mouse embryo at E12.5
- `seq_align`: Sequence alignment data
- `les_miserables`: Characters in Les Misérables
- `sc_multiomics`: Single-cell multi-omics dataset from COVID-19 patients
- `track`: Track data from GEO GSE137105 for MYC gene

Parameters
----------
name : str
    The name of the dataset
cache : bool
    Download data and cache locally

Returns
-------

classes.name: oncoprinter.core.OncoPrint
documentation:
OncoPrint plot

The oncoprint plot is a visualization for genomics data in cancer research.
It's first introduced by the cBioPortal project.
See <https://www.cbioportal.org/oncoprinter> for more details.

To use this class, import from oncoprinter

> ```pycon
> >>> from oncoprinter import OncoPrint
> ```

# Parameters

genomic_data
: Genomics data, each column is:
  : 1. Sample ID
    2. Track name
    3. Alteration

patients_order

: The order of samples, by default None

tracks_order

: The order of tracks, by default None

pieces

: Custom pieces for each alteration, by default None
  See {class}`Piece <marsilea.layers.Piece>` for details

background_color

: The background color

shrink

: The shrink ratio for each layer

width, height

: The size in inches to define the size of main canvas

aspect

: The aspect ratio of the main canvas

legend_kws

: The options for legend, by default None
  See {class}`cat_legend <legendkit.cat_legend>` for details

name

: The name of this OncoPrint

add_tracks_names

: The position to add tracks names
  If None, will not add tracks names

add_samples_names

: The position to add samples names
  If None, will not add samples names

add_mut_perc

: The position to add mutation percentage
  If None, will not add mutation percentage

add_tracks_counts

: The position to add tracks mutation counts
  If None, will not add tracks mutation counts

add_mut_counts

: The position to add mutation counts
  If None, will not add mutation counts

add_tracks_counts_size

: The size of tracks mutation counts

add_tracks_counts_pad

: The padding of tracks mutation counts

add_mut_counts_size

: The size of mutation counts

add_mut_counts_pad

: The padding of mutation counts

functions.name: marsilea.layout._split
documentation: Return the relative anchor point, in ratio
If not specify, we have few default setting
1. Size is inch unit
2. Origin point is left-bottom
Axes is a  rect, a rect can be recorded by a left-bottom anchor point
and width and height. When other axes is added, the anchor point is aligned
to either x-axis or y-axis, we could easily compute the final anchor point
if we know the size of added axes
It's unlikely to return axes right after being added, the split operation
is unknown, this will create more than one axes.

methods.name: marsilea.layout.CrossLayout.freeze
documentation:
Freeze the current layout and draw on figure

Freeze is safe to be called multiple times, it will update
the layout if draw on the same figure

# Parameters

figure

: A matplotlib figure instance

scale

: To scale the size of figure

\_debug

: Not a public parameter, for internal debug use,
  the information display on the canvas
  is not guarantee to be consistent.

# Returns

The cross-layout engine

This class implements the cross-layout. The axes are added
incrementally to the main axes.

- The size is always in the unit of inches


Parameters
----------
name : str
    The name of the main canvas
width : float
    The width of the main canvas
height : float
    The height of the main canvas
init_main : bool
    Add the main canvas as axes
projection : str
    The projection of the main canvas

Attributes
----------
is_composite : bool
    Use to indicate the current position of CrossLayout.
    Whether it's in a CompositeCrossLayout.

methods.name: marsilea.layout.StackCrossLayout.add_legend_ax
documentation:
Extend the layout

This is used to draw legends after concatenation

A stack of cross layouts

This class allow users to stack multiple cross layouts
either horizontally or vertically

Multiple StackCrossLayout can also be stacked

:::{warning}
This class are not supposed to be used directly by user
:::

# Parameters

layouts

: The layouts to be stacked

direction

: The direction of the stack, horizontal will stack from left to right
  vertical will stack from top to bottom

align

: The alignment of the stack, the default is center

methods.name: marsilea.base.LegendMaker.custom_legend
documentation:
Add a custom legend

Parameters
----------

legend_func : Callable
    A function that return the legend object,
    the legend must be an `Artist <matplotlib.artist.Artists>`
name : str, optional
    The name of the legend

methods.name: marsilea.base.LegendMaker.add_legends
documentation:
Draw legend based on the order of annotation

:::{note}
If you want to concatenate plots, please add legend after
concatenation, this will merge legends from every plots
:::

Stack is a pack of legends

# Parameters

side

: Which side to draw legend

pad

: The padding of the legend in inches

order

: The order of the legend, if None, the order will be the same as the order when adding plotters.
  You need to set name for each plotter when adding them, and specify the order here.

stack_by

: The direction to stack legends

stack_size

: The number of legends in a stack

align_legends

: The side to align legends in a stack

align_stacks

: The side to align stacks

legend_spacing

: The space between legends

stack_spacing

: The space between stacks

box_padding

: Add pad around the whole legend box
The factory class to handle legends

The base class that handle all rendering process

# Parameters

width

: The width of the main canvas in inches

height

: The height of the main canvas in inches

name

: The name of the main canvas

margin

: The margin of the main canvas in inches

init_main

: If True, the main canvas will be initialized

# See Also

{class}`~marsilea.base.ClusterBoard`

# Attributes

layout

: The layout manager

figure

: The matplotlib figure object

# Examples

Create a violin plot in white board

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 10)
    >>> h = ma.WhiteBoard(height=2)
    >>> h.add_layer(ma.plotter.Violin(data))
    >>> h.render()
```

classes.name: marsilea.base.ClusterBoard

methods.name: marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.add_dendrogram
documentation:
Run cluster and add dendrogram

:::{note}
1. method and metric only works when you
   add the first row/col dendrogram.
2. If `add_meta=False` and `add_base=False`, the dendrogram
   axes will not be created.
:::

# Parameters

side
method : str

> See scipy's {meth}`linkage <scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage>`

metric

: See scipy's {meth}`linkage <scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage>`

linkage

: Precomputed linkage matrix.
  See scipy's {meth}`linkage <scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage>` for
  specific format.

meta_linkage

: Precomputed chunk-level linkage matrix.
  See scipy's {meth}`linkage <scipy.cluster.hierarchy.linkage>` for
  specific format.

add_meta

: By default, add_meta is set to False if the linkage is provided, otherwise True.
  If the data is split, a meta dendrogram can be drawn for data
  chunks. The mean value of the data chunk is used to calculate
  linkage matrix for meta dendrogram.

add_base

: By default, add_meta is set to False if the linkage is provided, otherwise True.
  Draw the base dendrogram for each data chunk. You can turn this
  off if the base dendrogram is too crowded.

add_divider

: Draw a divide line that tells the difference between
  base and meta dendrogram.

divider_style

: The line style of the divide line

meta_color

: The color of the meta dendrogram

meta_ratio

: The size of meta dendrogram relative to the base dendrogram

linewidth

: The linewidth for every dendrogram and divide line

colors

: If only one color is specified, it will be applied to
  every dendrogram. If an array of color is specified, it will
  be applied to each base dendrogram.

show

: If False, the dendrogram will not be drawn and the axes will
  not be created.

name

: The name of the dendrogram axes

size : float
pad : float
get_meta_center: callable

> A function to calculate the centroid of data. It should accept a 2D numpy
> array as input and return a 1D numpy array of the same length as the number
> of columns in the input, representing the centroid. The default will use the
> mean values.

rasterized

: If True, the dendrogram will be rasterized

# Examples

You can change how the linkage matrix is calculated

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 11)
    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> h.add_dendrogram("left", method="ward", colors="green")
    >>> h.render()
```

Only show the meta dendrogram to avoid crowded dendrogram

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> h.cut_rows(cut=[4, 8])
    >>> h.add_dendrogram("left", add_base=False)
    >>> h.render()
```

Change color for each base dendrogram

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> h.cut_rows(cut=[4, 8])
    >>> h.add_dendrogram("left", colors=["#5470c6", "#91cc75", "#fac858"])
    >>> h.render()
```

methods.name: marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.hsplit
documentation:
Split the main canvas horizontally

:::{deprecated} 0.5.0 Use :meth:`~marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.cut_rows`             or :meth:`~marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.group_rows` instead
:::

# Parameters

cut

: The index of your data to specify where to split the canvas

labels

: The labels of your data, must be the same length as the data

order

: The order of the unique labels

spacing

: The spacing between each split chunks, default is 0.01

# Examples

Split the canvas by the unique labels

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 11)
    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> labels = ["A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A"]
    >>> h.hsplit(labels=labels, order=["A", "B", "C"])
    >>> h.add_left(ma.plotter.Labels(labels), pad=.1)
    >>> h.render()

```

Split the canvas by the index

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> h.hsplit(cut=[4, 8])
    >>> h.render()
```

methods.name: marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.vsplit
documentation:
Split the main canvas vertically

:::{deprecated} 0.5.0 Use :meth:`~marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.cut_cols`             or :meth:`~marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.group_cols` instead
:::

# Parameters

cut

: The index of your data to specify where to split the canvas

labels

: The labels of your data, must be the same length as the data

order

: The order of the unique labels

spacing

: The spacing between each split chunks, default is 0.01

# Examples

Split the canvas by the unique labels

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 11)
    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> labels = ["A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A", "B"]
    >>> h.vsplit(labels=labels, order=["A", "B", "C"])
    >>> h.add_top(ma.plotter.Labels(labels), pad=.1)
    >>> h.render()

```

Split the canvas by the index

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> h.vsplit(cut=[4, 8])
    >>> h.render()
```

methods.name: marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.group_rows
documentation:
Group rows into chunks

# Parameters

group

: The group of each row

order

: The order of the unique groups

spacing

: The spacing between each split chunks, default is 0.01

# Examples

Group rows by the unique labels

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 11)
    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> labels = ["A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A"]
    >>> h.group_rows(labels, order=["A", "B", "C"])
    >>> h.add_left(ma.plotter.Labels(labels), pad=0.1)
    >>> h.render()
```

methods.name: marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.group_cols
documentation:
Group columns into chunks

# Parameters

group

: The group of each column

order

: The order of the unique groups

spacing

: The spacing between each split chunks, default is 0.01

# Examples

Group columns by the unique labels

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> data = np.random.rand(11, 10)
    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> labels = ["A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A", "B", "C", "A"]
    >>> h.group_cols(labels, order=["A", "B", "C"])
    >>> h.add_top(ma.plotter.Labels(labels), pad=0.1)
    >>> h.render()
```

methods.name: marsilea.base.ClusterBoard.cut_rows
documentation:
Cut the main canvas by rows

# Parameters

cut

: The index of your data to specify where to cut the canvas

spacing

: The spacing between each cut, default is 0.01

# Examples

Cut the canvas by the index

```
.. plot::
    :context: close-figs

    >>> data = np.random.rand(10, 11)
    >>> import marsilea as ma
    >>> h = ma.Heatmap(data)
    >>> h.cut_rows([4, 8])
    >>> h.render()
```
